East Asian Traditions

Wang Yangming

1472 – 1529

Innate knowing and the unity of knowledge and action.

Wang Yangming, 王陽明, also known as Wang Shouren, 王守仁, 1472–1529, is best understood not as a “philosopher of mind” in the post-Cartesian disciplinary sense, but as one of the great philosophers of heart-mind, 心, xin: a single psycho-ethical term covering cognition, affect, intention, desire, moral orientation, and action. In early Chinese philosophy, xin is not merely an inner faculty of representation; it is the directing center of the person, credited with thinking, understanding, knowing, intending, desiring, and feeling. That is why “heart-mind” is usually a better translation than simply “mind.”

The central reconstructed thesis is this: for Wang, mind is not an interior container of representations opposed to an exterior world; it is the morally intelligent, affectively charged, world-involving activity through which things become significant, action-guiding, and ethically ordered. The original structure of this mind is liangzhi, 良知, “innate knowing” or “pure moral knowing.” Its pathology is obstruction by selfish desire, 私欲, which fragments mind from world, knowledge from action, and self from others. Its remedy is zhi liangzhi, 致良知, “extending” or “fully realizing” innate knowing in concrete affairs.

This report treats Wang as a major alternative genealogy for philosophy of mind: one that begins not from the mind-body problem, but from the relation among moral perception, affect, motivation, action, selfhood, and world-disclosure.

1. Historical position: Wang before the Cartesian problem

Wang lived in Ming China, more than half a century before Descartes. He was a scholar-official, general, and Neo-Confucian philosopher, and he became one of the leading critics of the orthodox Cheng-Zhu school associated with Zhu Xi, 朱熹, 1130–1200. He is especially known for the doctrine of the “unity of knowing and acting,” 知行合一, which the Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy describes as interpretable as a denial of weakness of will.

This chronology matters. Western philosophy of mind is often narrated from Descartes onward: mind and body become two distinct substances, characterized respectively by thought and extension, and this dualist formulation becomes a standard starting point for the modern mind-body problem. Wang’s philosophy develops before that Cartesian framing and does not take as primary the question “How can an immaterial mind interact with a material body?” His problem is different: how can the heart-mind remain faithful to its original moral lucidity while acting in the concrete world?

The background is Song-Ming Neo-Confucianism. Zhu Xi’s synthesis held that everything has li, 理, “pattern” or “principle,” and qi, 氣, the concrete psychophysical stuff through which things exist. Human beings possess the complete pattern, but their qi may be clear or turbid; ethical cultivation clarifies qi so that the pattern can manifest more fully. Wang accepts much of this conceptual framework, but relocates the center of gravity. For Zhu Xi, the learner “investigates things” in order to grasp their pattern. For Wang, seeking principle “outside” the heart-mind misconstrues the nature of moral knowing. The principle is not a detached object to be discovered after inquiry; it is present in the heart-mind’s original, properly responsive activity.

Wang’s own biography is philosophically important. As a young man, he tried to follow what he took to be Zhu Xi’s method of “investigating things” by contemplating bamboo, hoping to discover its principle. The episode ended in exhaustion and illness, and later became a symbol of the failure of externalized moral inquiry. His decisive awakening came during exile in Guizhou after political punishment; he later expressed the insight by saying, in effect, that the root of transformation lies in the mind and that he had previously searched outside among “branches and leaves.”

His major philosophical material comes from teaching dialogues, letters, and works such as the Chuanxi lu, 傳習錄, often translated as Instructions for Practical Living or Record for Practice, and the late Inquiry on the Great Learning. These were not composed as systematic treatises in the Western style; they emerged from pedagogy, official life, military action, and discipleship. That matters because Wang’s philosophy of mind is itself practical: its truth is not separable from the discipline of living it.

2. The conceptual grammar: mind, principle, intention, thing, and innate knowing

The key terms must be handled carefully.

Xin, 心, heart-mind, is the master of the person. In Wang’s own words, as preserved in the Chinese text, “the master of the body is the mind; what the mind issues forth is intention; the substance of intention is knowing; where intention is directed is the thing.” This line is crucial because it collapses several distinctions that later Western philosophy often separates: subject, intention, object, cognition, and action are not independent atoms but phases of one dynamic field.

Li, 理, pattern or principle, is not merely a law of nature in the modern scientific sense. It is normative coherence: the way things ought to be ordered, the intelligible structure of right response. Wang’s formula “mind is principle,” 心即理, does not mean that private opinion creates truth. It means that the heart-mind in its original state is already structured by normative coherence. The divergence from Zhu Xi is sharp: for Zhu, the heart-mind comes to know pattern as something to which it relates; for Wang, pattern resides in the heart-mind’s original responses rather than as an independent object standing over against the mind.

Yi, 意, intention or inclination, is the heart-mind’s concrete directedness. It is not merely a neutral thought. It is already an orientation toward acting, valuing, pursuing, avoiding, approving, or rejecting. That is why Wang can say that where intention is directed, there is the “thing,” 物. “Thing” here often means not a bare object but an affair, a morally relevant situation: serving one’s parent, serving one’s ruler, caring for people, responding through seeing, hearing, speech, and movement.

Ge wu, 格物, traditionally rendered by Zhu Xi as “investigating things,” is reinterpreted by Wang as rectifying affairs at the point where intention is engaged. In the Great Learning, “extending knowledge” lies in ge wu. Zhu Xi took this to involve encountering things and exhaustively investigating their principle; Wang reinterprets it as a discipline of the heart-mind’s intention in concrete affairs.

Liangzhi, 良知, is Wang’s signature concept. It is often translated as “innate knowledge,” but “innate moral knowing,” “pure knowing,” or “original moral discernment” may be more precise. It is not a storehouse of factual propositions. It is the heart-mind’s immediate capacity to know the ethical quality of one’s intentions and situations. Wang says that the mind naturally knows: seeing one’s father, it knows filiality; seeing one’s elder brother, it knows respect; seeing a child about to fall into a well, it knows compassion.

Contemporary scholarship has emphasized that Wang develops liangzhi beyond its Mencian source. In the Mencius, liangzhi and liangneng, “innate ability,” are connected with people’s innate recognition that they should love parents and respect elder brothers. Wang elevates liangzhi into a central account of how the heart-mind knows the rightness or wrongness of its own inclinations.

3. Wang’s core move: from epistemology to moral phenomenology

Wang’s philosophy begins by refusing a picture of mind as a spectator looking outward at a world of independently intelligible objects. He does not deny that there are mountains, trees, bodies, texts, rituals, or social institutions. Rather, he denies that their moral significance is intelligible as something wholly external to the heart-mind.

His statement that there is “no principle outside the mind” and “no thing outside the mind” is therefore best read as a claim about lived, value-laden disclosure. Things appear as significant within the activity of the heart-mind. The “thing” is not simply an object with coordinates; it is the affair as taken up by intention. When the mind is directed toward serving a parent, that filial affair is the relevant “thing.” When it is directed toward speech, perception, ritual, governance, or care, those become things in the morally salient sense.

This is why Wang should not be assimilated too quickly to Berkeleyan subjective idealism. Recent scholarship explicitly rejects the reading that Wang’s liangzhi entails subjective idealism, comparing it instead to something like transcendental subjectivity: not an empirical ego that invents objects, but a condition of meaningful appearing. Other recent scholarship argues that Wang is not a subjective idealist like Berkeley, while still allowing a broader “universal mind” or ontological idealist reading because Wang sometimes identifies mind with Dao, Heaven, and the all-encompassing order.

So the most careful formulation is this: Wang is not a solipsist. He is a normative-phenomenological idealist, or a moral idealist of heart-mind. The mind does not manufacture the physical world; it discloses the world as an ethically charged field of response. The world is not denied. The idea of a morally neutral world “outside” the heart-mind is denied.

4. Knowledge and action: Wang’s most radical philosophy of mind thesis

The doctrine of the unity of knowing and acting, 知行合一, is the best-known expression of Wang’s philosophy. It is also the key to extracting a philosophy of mind from him.

The ordinary view says: first we know, then we decide, then we act. Wang thinks this sequence falsifies moral psychology. In genuine moral knowing, knowledge is already active. To know filiality is not merely to be able to state “one should be filial.” To know filiality is to be disposed, moved, and oriented toward filial action in the relevant situation. If someone says they know filiality but does not act filially, Wang says this is not genuine knowing; selfish desire has already severed knowing from its original state.

Wang’s own formulation is striking: knowledge is the beginning of action, and action is the completion of knowledge. If one understands the point, speaking only of knowledge already includes action, and speaking only of action already includes knowledge.

This is not a crude denial that people can recite moral propositions without acting. Wang knows they can. His claim is that such recitation is not true moral knowing, 真知. A person can possess verbal, theoretical, or conventional cognition while lacking actual moral knowledge. The unity thesis is therefore not a theory about all information; it is a theory about genuine practical-moral cognition.

The Great Learning examples are central. Wang says that seeing a lovely sight and loving it, or smelling a hateful odor and hating it, are not two discrete events joined by inference. The response is internal to the perception. Similarly, in moral life, true recognition is already affective and action-guiding.

This is where Wang enters the global history of debates about weakness of will. In Western terms, he is close to the Socratic denial of akrasia: if one truly knows the good, one cannot simply fail to act accordingly. The philosophical tradition has long debated whether weakness of will is possible, and Plato’s Socrates famously denied it. Aristotle, by contrast, gives a more complex account in which a person may deliberate correctly and yet act under passion; he distinguishes weakness from impetuosity. Wang’s position is closer to Socratic intellectualism than to ordinary Aristotelian akrasia, but with a Confucian difference: the relevant knowing is not abstract rational calculation but the heart-mind’s morally affective responsiveness.

A sophisticated interpretation should distinguish three levels in Wang’s doctrine.

First, as a normative claim, genuine moral knowledge should be embodied in conduct.

Second, as a phenomenological claim, moral perception is not cold representation plus later motivation; it is already evaluative and motivational.

Third, as a pedagogical claim, separating knowing and acting produces “bookish” moral failure: people defer ethical transformation indefinitely while accumulating discourse, commentary, and reputation. Wang explicitly says his teaching is a “medicine” for the disease of separating knowledge and action.

Modern scholarship has similarly noted that Wang’s unity doctrine functions both as an account of the original relation of knowledge and action and as a practical remedy for defective learning.

5. Liangzhi as moral perception, conscience, and self-knowledge

Liangzhi is often compared to conscience, but that comparison is both useful and insufficient. Like conscience, it registers the moral quality of one’s own intentions. Unlike many modern accounts of conscience, it is not merely a subjective feeling of guilt or approval. It is an original knowing that has cognitive, affective, and motivational dimensions.

Contemporary scholarship emphasizes that Wang’s liangzhi knows whether one’s motivating concerns are right or wrong. It is not merely capable of knowing; Wang often speaks as if it invariably knows when an intention or concern has arisen and whether it is ethically sound.

This gives Wang a distinctive answer to the question “How does the mind know itself?” He does not treat self-knowledge primarily as introspective access to private mental states. Instead, self-knowledge is the heart-mind’s immediate moral awareness of its own directedness. The crucial object of self-knowledge is not “I am having a mental representation” but “this intention is right,” “this concern is selfish,” “this response is compassionate,” or “this avoidance is cowardly.”

Here Wang’s theory becomes especially relevant to contemporary philosophy of mind. Modern intentionality theory often defines intentionality as the mind’s capacity to be about, represent, or stand for objects, properties, and states of affairs. Wang’s account of intention is not representational in that narrow sense. It is not simply that a mental state has content. Rather, a heart-mind event is directed toward an affair in a way that is already value-laden and practically poised. Wang’s yi is closer to concernful directedness than to neutral representation.

The result is a moral phenomenology of attention. The heart-mind is always somewhere: with a parent, with reputation, with profit, with anger, with service, with fear, with ritual, with justice. Wherever it is, liangzhi knows the ethical quality of that placement. The task is not to acquire an external moral theory but to stop evading the lucid knowledge already operating in one’s heart-mind.

6. The Four-Sentence Teaching: mind-substance, intention, knowing, rectification

Near the end of Wang’s life, the famous “Four-Sentence Teaching,” 四句教, became a compressed statement of his doctrine. In paraphrase: the substance of the mind is beyond fixed good and evil; the movement of intention has good and evil; knowing good and evil is liangzhi; doing good and removing evil is ge wu. Wang warns his disciples not to absolutize one side of this formula. Some students need direct awakening to the original mind; others need concrete discipline at the level of intention.

This is a subtle position. “The mind-substance is without good and evil” does not mean moral nihilism. It means that the original heart-mind, before distorted partiality, is not one item within the field of moral alternatives. It is the luminous condition through which good and evil are known. But once intention moves, moral differentiation appears. Therefore, the work of cultivation occurs not in abstract contemplation of a pure mind but in the concrete correction of intentions.

Wang is acutely aware of a danger: if one speaks only of original purity, people may lapse into empty quietism. He says that people with habitual minds must be taught to do the real work of doing good and removing evil; otherwise they cultivate a vacant stillness detached from actual affairs.

This is an important safeguard against misreading Wang as a mystical subjectivist. His doctrine of mind is not “whatever my mind feels is right.” It is disciplined, demanding, and actional. The test of liangzhi is not private certainty but the elimination of selfish obstruction in concrete affairs.

7. Mind and world: “nothing outside the mind” without solipsism

Wang’s most controversial thesis is that there is no principle, thing, or affair outside the mind. A crude interpretation says: Wang believes objects do not exist unless perceived. That reading is weak.

A better reading begins from his definition of “thing” as the place of intention. The morally salient world is not a set of inert objects but a field of affairs: parent-child, ruler-minister, friend-friend, seeing-hearing-speaking-moving, serving, learning, governing, responding. These are not outside the heart-mind because they are constituted as meaningful affairs by the heart-mind’s directed involvement.

Recent scholarship on Wang’s famous “blossoming tree” dialogue argues that the mind’s role is not simply conceptualization or categorization but appreciation and value-perception. On this interpretation, when Wang says the flowers and the mind “brighten” together, he is not denying botanical existence but emphasizing that their presence as meaningful, beautiful, value-laden phenomena occurs in relation to mind.

This puts Wang surprisingly near later phenomenological concerns. Phenomenology studies structures of consciousness from the first-person point of view, especially intentionality: the directedness of experience toward something. It also studies the meanings things have in experience, including objects, events, tools, self, others, action, and the life-world. Wang’s “no thing outside the mind” is not Husserlian phenomenology, but it has a family resemblance: the world that matters for human life is the world as meaningfully disclosed in experience and action.

The difference is equally important. Husserlian phenomenology can bracket existence and analyze structures of consciousness. Wang’s inquiry is not neutral description. It is ethically charged from the start. To disclose the world truly is to respond to it rightly.

8. Selfhood: from private ego to “one body” with Heaven, Earth, and the myriad things

Wang’s philosophy of mind is inseparable from a relational theory of self. The virtuous person recognizes that their heart-mind forms “one body” with Heaven, Earth, and the myriad things. The vicious person treats self and others as fundamentally separate because they are blocked by the physical boundary of the body and by selfish desire.

This “one body” thesis is not a decorative metaphysical flourish. It grounds benevolence, 仁, ren. Wang uses Mencius’s example of the child about to fall into a well: human beings naturally feel alarm and compassion. He extends this beyond humans to animals, plants, and even damaged tiles and stones, arguing that the scope of concern reveals a deeper unity of mind and world.

The argument is vulnerable. The Stanford Encyclopedia notes that it is not obvious everyone feels concern for damaged stones or tiles, and it is not clear that Wang’s metaphysics is the best explanation for such reactions even if they occur. Evolutionary or psychological explanations might account for compassion toward humans, animals, and living things.

Still, philosophically, Wang’s selfhood is profound. The self is not a private Cartesian interior. It is a node in a morally structured field. To become good is not to impose moral rules on an alien world; it is to recover felt participation in a world of which one is already a part.

This makes Wang relevant to contemporary debates over embodied and embedded cognition. Embodied cognition challenges the idea that cognition is only computation in the brain and instead emphasizes the body and the body’s interactions with the environment as constitutive or contributory to cognition. Wang is not doing cognitive science, but he offers a premodern moral analogue: mind is not an isolated inner processor; it lives through bodily perception, social roles, action, and responsive participation in the world.

9. Cultivation: the mind cures itself in affairs

Wang’s self-cultivation is neither bookish scholasticism nor passive meditation. It has several dimensions.

First, there is sincerity of intention, 誠意. Since intention is where the heart-mind concretely moves, cultivation must begin where intention arises. Wang says that if an intention is directed toward doing good, one should actually do good; if directed toward removing evil, one should actually remove it. Extending knowledge is not “empty” cognition but rectification in real affairs.

Second, there is vigilant attention to incipient thoughts and concerns. Wang’s unity doctrine makes even the first stirring of an intention morally important. If a selfish thought arises, one should not excuse it on the ground that no external deed has yet occurred. For Wang, the arising of the intention is already part of action.

Third, there is study, but study is subordinated to realization. Wang does not reject books, classics, ritual, or inquiry. He rejects their separation from transformation. His critique is of learning that accumulates discourse while leaving selfish intention intact. The point of classics is to illuminate the heart-mind’s original moral structure, not to create a substitute for it.

Fourth, there is quiet-sitting, but Wang resists quietism. He recommends calming and collecting the mind for beginners whose thoughts are scattered, but he warns that empty stillness without active correction is useless. The real work is the removal of selfish desire and the preservation of heavenly principle in affairs.

Fifth, there is public and political action. Wang’s own teaching occurred amid official duties, military campaigns, and governance. He was not a recluse. This biographical fact matters because his theory of knowledge-action unity is lived in public service, not merely in private spirituality.

10. Comparison with Western philosophy of mind

Wang’s place in the larger field is best seen not by forcing him into Western categories, but by comparing problematics.

With Plato’s Socrates, Wang shares an anti-akratic tendency: true knowing cannot be inert before action. But Wang’s “knowing” is not primarily dialectical grasp of definitions. It is liangzhi, a morally affective, situation-sensitive discernment. He therefore transforms intellectualism into heart-mind moral phenomenology.

With Aristotle, Wang shares the idea that practical knowledge is inseparable from action and character. Aristotle’s ethics treats practical wisdom, perception of particulars, habituation, and action as central; he also gives a nuanced account of akrasia. Wang is less tolerant than Aristotle of separating correct judgment from action, because for Wang a failure to act shows that selfish desire has already blocked genuine knowing.

With Descartes, the contrast is fundamental. Descartes’s modern legacy includes substance dualism, with mind and body as distinct substances, and a theory of innate intellectual content whose source lies in the nature of the mind. Wang also affirms something innate, but his innateness is not primarily mathematical, logical, or metaphysical content. It is moral-practical discernment. Nor is his mind a separate immaterial substance. The heart-mind is affective, bodily, social, and world-involving.

With Spinoza, there are partial convergences around monism and the rejection of Cartesian separation. Spinoza’s philosophy of mind is governed by universal intelligibility, explanatory closure of the mental and physical, and the priority of an infinite thinker over finite minds. Wang’s “one body” thesis also resists atomistic separateness. But Spinoza’s system is geometric, deterministic, and explanatory; Wang’s is moral-cultivational and focused on the recovery of innate moral responsiveness.

With Hume and moral sentimentalism, Wang shares the refusal to make moral cognition a purely detached rational process. Kant’s SEP entry notes the empiricist claim, associated with Hume, that only feelings can motivate action and reason cannot be the source of moral motivation. Wang would reject the dichotomy. Liangzhi is both cognitive and affective; it knows and moves. Moral feeling is not non-rational sentiment, and moral knowing is not motivationless judgment.

With Kant, Wang shares the idea that moral authority is internal rather than externally imposed. Kant grounds morality in autonomous practical reason and the categorical imperative; he holds that self-governing reason gives persons equal worth and respect. Wang grounds moral authority in the original heart-mind and its liangzhi. But Wang is not Kantian. He does not derive morality from formal universal law, does not separate noumenal freedom from phenomenal causality in Kant’s way, and does not make morality primarily a priori legislation. His morality is immediate, situational, and rooted in cultivated responsiveness.

With phenomenology, Wang converges in treating mind as directed, meaningful, and world-involving. Phenomenology studies consciousness from the first-person point of view and treats intentionality as central. Wang’s line that where intention is directed, there is the thing, can be read as a moral-phenomenological claim: an affair becomes salient through the heart-mind’s directed concern. The difference is that Wang’s intentionality is not neutral about values. It is already answerable to heavenly principle.

With pragmatism, especially Peirce and James, Wang shares an anti-spectator conception of knowing. Pragmatism clarifies concepts by their practical bearings and develops an anti-Cartesian outlook on inquiry. Wang likewise refuses to detach knowledge from practice. But pragmatism is generally fallibilist, experimental, and oriented toward inquiry; Wang’s view is a moral-realization theory centered on recovering an innate ethical source.

With contemporary embodied, embedded, and enactive approaches, Wang shares the rejection of mind as merely inner representation. Embodied cognition emphasizes the body and organism-environment interaction in cognition. Wang’s philosophy offers a normative version of that insight: the heart-mind knows in and through its situated activity. Still, Wang should not be retroactively converted into a cognitive scientist. His primary concern is sagehood, not empirical explanation.

11. Wang’s influence and later criticism

Wang, together with Lu Xiangshan, 陸象山, is regarded as a founder of the Lu-Wang School, or School of Mind, one of the two great wings of Neo-Confucianism alongside the Cheng-Zhu School, or School of Principle. His thought influenced China and Japan; in Japan it became known as Ōyōmeigaku, and adherents included Nakae Tōju and Kumazawa Banzan. It also inspired some figures connected with the Meiji Restoration.

His later reception was not uniformly positive. Qing evidential scholars criticized Song-Ming Confucians for “empty words,” and Dai Zhen criticized thinkers like Wang for encouraging people to treat subjective opinion as infallible moral sense, projecting Buddhist concepts onto Confucian classics, and neglecting ordinary physical desires.

Those criticisms remain philosophically important. Wang’s system has real dangers. If liangzhi is misunderstood, it can license moral arrogance: “my intuition is heavenly principle.” If “mind is principle” is detached from discipline, it can become subjectivism. If knowledge and action are identified too strongly, the theory may underdescribe weakness, compulsion, trauma, social pressure, and self-deception. If the “one body” thesis is universalized too quickly, it may rest on questionable empirical claims about human compassion.

But Wang has resources against these dangers. He repeatedly insists on removing selfish desire, correcting intentions, acting in concrete affairs, and avoiding empty quietism. He does not say that any spontaneous impulse is good. He says that the original heart-mind, when unobstructed, knows; therefore the hard task is to remove obstruction rather than baptize impulse.

12. A reconstructed Wangian philosophy of mind

If we extract a full philosophy of mind from Wang, it can be stated as follows.

Ontology of mind: Mind is not a private inner substance. It is the living, luminous, morally structured activity of the person, continuous with body, world, social role, and cosmic order. Its deepest structure is not representation but responsive normativity.

Mind-world relation: The world is not outside mind in the morally relevant sense because things are disclosed as affairs through intention. A bare object becomes a humanly significant “thing” when the heart-mind is directed toward it. This does not abolish external existence; it denies that moral meaning is intelligible apart from heart-mind involvement.

Selfhood: The self is relational and porous. Its true form is “one body” with others and with the world. Ethical failure is a contraction of selfhood under the illusion of separateness.

Cognition: Genuine cognition, especially moral cognition, is not neutral representation plus later evaluation. It is immediate, affective, and practical discernment. Liangzhi knows the moral quality of intentions and affairs.

Affect: Affect is not an irrational intruder into cognition. Love, shame, compassion, aversion, respect, and concern can be modes of knowing. The analogy with loving a beautiful sight or hating a bad odor shows that response is internal to recognition.

Intention: Intention is the heart-mind’s concrete directedness. It is where mind becomes morally assessable. A thought is not harmless merely because it has not yet become visible behavior; intention is already incipient action.

Action: Action is not an external appendage to knowledge. It is knowledge’s completion. Genuine knowing is enacted; action is the manifestation of knowing.

Error and pathology: Error arises when selfish desire blocks liangzhi. The pathology is fragmentation: mind versus world, knowledge versus action, self versus others, study versus practice.

Cultivation: Cultivation is the extension of liangzhi into every affair. It includes attention, sincerity, study, quieting, correction, ritual, service, and public action. Its aim is not acquisition of external moral data but unobstructed functioning of original moral knowing.

Political implication: Good governance depends on cultivated heart-minds. Since Wang’s own life joined philosophy, administration, and military command, his philosophy implies that public order is inseparable from moral psychology.

Epistemic standard: The standard is not private certainty but purified responsiveness. One must ask: is this intention blocked by selfish desire, or does it manifest the heart-mind’s original coherence with heavenly principle?

13. Why Wang matters for philosophy of mind

Wang’s importance lies in offering an alternative to several entrenched modern divisions.

He dissolves the division between cognition and affect. He dissolves the division between moral knowledge and motivation. He dissolves the division between self and world as independent starting points. He dissolves the division between philosophy and practice. He also offers a theory of mind in which ethical life is not a later application of psychology but the very place where mind’s nature is revealed.

For contemporary philosophy, Wang is especially valuable because he shows how a philosophy of mind can begin from moral agency rather than representation, from world-involving action rather than inner mental objects, and from self-cultivation rather than detached explanation. His philosophy is not a replacement for modern analytic philosophy of mind, phenomenology, moral psychology, or cognitive science. It is a powerful alternative architecture: a heart-mind philosophy in which consciousness, value, and action are originally unified.

The shortest rigorous summary is this: Wang Yangming’s mind is a morally luminous, affectively responsive, action-realizing field of world-involvement. To know is to be already answerable; to be answerable is to act; to act rightly is to let the original heart-mind disclose the world as one body.

Digital Minds · A history of thinking about the mind, from antiquity to the present day.
A personal research project by Fabián. · About · Glossary
